Green|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/players/blake-green/summary.html|publisher=Rugby League Project}}[http://203.166.101.37/NRL08/playercareer.asp?lplayer=1590100 NRL Stats]{{dead link|date=July 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} |updated = 26 May 2021 }} '''Blake Green''' (born 18 September 1986) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|fe}} and {{rlp|hb}} in the 2000s, 2010s, and 2020s. Green was regarded as somewhat of a [[Journeyman (sports)|journeyman]] during his career after playing for nine separate clubs. He played for the [[Parramatta Eels]], [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] and [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] in the [[National Rugby League]], [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] and the [[Wigan Warriors]] in the [[Super League]], before returning to the NRL with the [[Melbourne Storm]], [[Manly Warringah Sea Eagles]], [[New Zealand Warriors]] and finally [[Newcastle Knights]]. He is one of only three players in the history of the game (the others being [[Darrien Doherty]] and [[Tyran Smith]]) to have the distinction of playing first grade for seven different NRL clubs. Green won the [[Harry Sunderland Trophy]] as man-of-the-match in the [[2013 Super League Grand Final]] whilst playing for the [[Wigan Warriors]]. ==Background== Green was born in [[Fairfield, New South Wales]], Australia. He played junior rugby league for [[Cabramatta Two Blues]] and [[Hills District Bulls]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.nswrl.com.au/news/2018/04/01/green-in-origin-frame-after-warriors-win/|title=Green in Origin Frame after Warriors Win|date=31 March 2018|website=New South Wales Rugby League}} Blake was educated at [[Westfields Sports High School]] from 1999–2004 and played for and captained the [[Australia national schoolboys rugby league team|2004 Australian Schoolboys]].{{cite web|title=SportingPulse Homepage for Australian Secondary Schools Rugby League|url=http://www.sportingpulse.com/assoc_page.cgi?c=7-2130-0-0-0&sID=26424|access-date=10 October 2008|publisher=SportingPulse|archive-date=31 January 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170131030043/http://websites.sportstg.com/assoc_page.cgi?c=7-2130-0-0-0&sID=26424|url-status=dead}} ==Playing career== Green made his professional rugby league début during the 2007 NRL season with the [[Parramatta Eels]]. He was then signed up by the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] on a two-year deal. [[File:Blake Green.jpg|thumb|right|Green playing for the Sharks in 2009]] He moved to the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] in 2010, enjoying his most consistent season to that point, playing 18 games in the halves. In October 2010 it was announced that Green had signed a two-year deal with the [[East Riding of Yorkshire]] club [[Hull Kingston Rovers]], starting from the 2011 Super League season. Green linked up with former teammate [[Michael Dobson (rugby league)|Michael Dobson]] to form a new partnership at half-back for the Robins.{{citation needed|date=July 2019}} He played in the [[2013 Challenge Cup Final]] victory over [[Hull F.C.]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite web|url= https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/23794947|title= Hull FC 0–16 Wigan Warriors|date=24 August 2013|work= BBC Sport|access-date=13 November 2019}}{{cite web|url= https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2013/aug/24/hull-fc-wigan-warriors-challenge-cup-final|title= Wigan Warriors overcome Hull FC and elements to win Challenge Cup|date=24 August 2013|work= Guardian|access-date=13 November 2019}}{{cite web|url= http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,,11070_3660839,00.html|title= Wigan Warriors grind out victory over Hull|date=24 August 2013|work= Sky Sports|access-date=13 November 2019}} Green won the [[Harry Sunderland Trophy]] in the [[2013 Super League Grand Final]] against the [[Warrington Wolves]] on 5 October 2013 as he helped the Wigan Warriors come from 16–2 down to win 30–16 as he scored the try which moved the Wigan Warriors 24–16 ahead in a crucial stage of the game, and put on a fantastic display at stand off despite taking a cheap-shot on the ground from Warrington Wolves' [[Ben Westwood]] which left Green on the ground out cold for a few minutes with a black eye and fractured eye socket at [[Old Trafford]].{{cite web|title=Super League Grand Final |url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/super_league/grandfinal |publisher=Super League |access-date=14 July 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130729050226/http://www.superleague.co.uk/super_league/grandfinal |archive-date=29 July 2013}}{{cite news|last=Newsum|first=Matt|title=Super League Grand Final: Wigan Warriors beat Warrington|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/24384930|access-date=13 November 2019|newspaper=BBC Sport|date=5 October 2013}}{{cite web|title=Super League Grand Final: Warrington v Wigan|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/rugby-league/24339698|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131007163603/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/rugby-league/24339698|url-status=dead|archive-date=7 October 2013|work=BBC Sport|access-date=13 November 2019}}{{cite web|url = https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/10358228/Super-League-Grand-Final-2013-Warrington-Wolves-16-Wigan-Warriors-30-match-report.html|title= Warrington Wolves 16 Wigan Warriors 30|date=5 October 2013|work=Daily Telegraph|access-date=13 November 2019}}{{cite web|url = https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2013/oct/05/warrington-wigan-super-league-grand-final|title= Wigan see off Warrington in X-rated Grand Final to complete double|date=5 October 2013|work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=13 November 2019}} On 10 June 2014, Green announced he would leave the Wigan Warriors at the end of the season.{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article/30422/green-set-to-leave-wigan|title=RUGBY-LEAGUE.COM|access-date=25 August 2016}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12215/9343969/super-league-blake-green-to-leave-wigan-warriors-at-the-end-of-the-season|title=Super League: Blake Green to leave Wigan Warriors at the end of the season|website=Sky Sports}} He played in the [[2014 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] at [[Old Trafford]].{{cite web|url = https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/11156623/St-Helens-14-Wigan-Warriors-6-Moment-of-madness-from-Wales-international-Ben-Flower-costs-Wigan-dear.html|title= St Helens 14 Wigan Warriors 6: Moment of madness from Wales international Ben Flower costs Wigan dear|date= 11 October 2014 |work=Daily Telegraph|access-date=15 October 2014}}{{cite web|url = https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/oct/11/st-helens-wigan-super-league-grand-final-match-report|title= St Helens win Grand Final after Wigan's Ben Flower is sent off|date= 11 October 2014 |work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=15 October 2014}}{{cite web|url = https://www.bbc.com/sport/0/rugby-league/29559845|title= St Helens 14–6 Wigan Warriors|date= 11 October 2014 |work=BBC Sport|access-date=15 October 2014}} On returning to the NRL he signed with Melbourne in 2015. In the [[2016 NRL season]], Green played nearly every match for Melbourne as the club claimed the [[Minor Premiership]]. Green played for Melbourne in the [[2016 NRL Grand Final]] where they lost 14-12 to Cronulla-Sutherland at [[ANZ Stadium]].{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2016/oct/02/nrl-grand-final-2016-cronulla-sharks-vs-melbourne-storm-live/|title=Cronulla defeat Storm to break 50-year NRL grand final hoodoo - as it happened|website=[[The Guardian]] }} Green then signed with the [[Manly Warringah Sea Eagles]] for a two-year deal, forming a halves combination with [[Daly Cherry-Evans]]. On 20 November 2017, Green was released from the second and final year of his contract with Manly to join the [[New Zealand Warriors]] on a three-year deal.{{cite web|last=Skipwith|first=David|date=20 November 2017|title=Blake Green signs with Warriors|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=11946019|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |access-date=26 May 2018}}{{cite news|date=21 November 2017|title=Blake Green signs three-year deal with New Zealand Warriors|url=http://www.news.com.au/sport/nrl/warriors/blake-green-signs-three-year-deal-with-new-zealand-warriors/news-story/b717a235d21539e3d4ce7d114ea2befb|work=news.com.au|access-date=26 May 2018}} On 4 August 2020, Green joined the [[Newcastle Knights]] mid-season to play out the rest of the 2020 season.{{cite news|date=4 August 2020|title=Blake Green joins Knights after Warriors release|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2020/08/04/veteran-playmaker-blake-green-set-to-join-newcastle-knights/|work=NRL.com}} Upon joining Newcastle, Green equalled both [[Darrien Doherty]] and [[Tyran Smith]]'s record for most [[NRL]] club's played for with Newcastle being his seventh club.{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/nrl-2020-blake-green-biggest-journeyman-knights-warriors-sea-eagles-storm-bulldogs-sharks-eels/news-story/18687f53e9fb57e7e94279b23b4570cc/|title=Blake Green journeyman|website=www.foxsports.com.au}} He played his first game for the club in round 13 against the [[Wests Tigers]], with the Knights winning the game 44-4.{{Cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/seasons/nrl-2020/round-13/newcastle-vs-wests-tigers/summary.html|title=NRL 2020 - Round 13 - Rugby League Project|website=www.rugbyleagueproject.org}} During his third game for the Knights against the [[North Queensland Cowboys]], Green ruptured his [[Anterior cruciate ligament injury|anterior cruciate ligament]], ending his season.{{Cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/nrl-2020-blake-green-contract-canterburybankstown-bulldogs-kieran-foran/news-story/f8d868c3ace3f8969689974f8a8d9c88|title=NRL 2020: Blake Green contract, Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs, Kieran Foran|date=26 August 2020}} On 26 August 2020, Green signed a one-year contract with the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] for the 2021 season.{{cite web|url= https://wwos.nine.com.au/nrl/bulldogs-sign-blake-green-kieran-foran-replacement/a3e69884-400a-4fe1-8e7b-e2c0d988603d|title=Bulldogs sign Blake Green |website= wwos.nine.com.au}} He was then released from his contract with Canterbury-Bankstown, after they had signed [[Kyle Flanagan (rugby league)|Kyle Flanagan]], with Green returning to Newcastle on a one-year deal for 2021.{{cite web|url= https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/7003560/knights-re-sign-veteran-playmaker-blake-green-for-2021/?cs=2503|title=Knights re-sign veteran playmaker Blake Green for 2021|website= www.newcastleherald.com.au}} Green made his return to the field for the Newcastle club in round 4 of the [[2021 NRL season|2021 season]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/Custom/MTUtMjEyNS00LS0tLS0tNTU3My0tLS0tLS0tLS0tLS0=|title=Custom Match List - Rugby League Project|website=www.rugbyleagueproject.org}} He played in five more games before announcing his immediate retirement from rugby league.{{Cite web|url=https://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news/2021/05/25/blake-green-confirms-retirement-from-rugby-league|title=Blake Green confirms retirement from Rugby League|date=25 May 2021|website=Newcastle Knights}} ===Honours=== 2004 – [[Australia national schoolboys rugby league team]]
2013 – Winner [[2013 Super League Grand Final|Super League Grand Final]] – [[Wigan Warriors]]
2013 – Winner [[Harry Sunderland Trophy]] – [[Wigan Warriors]]
2013 – Winner [[2013 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]] – [[Wigan Warriors]]
2014 – Runner-up [[2014 Super League Grand Final|Super League Grand Final]] – [[Wigan Warriors]]
2016 – [[2016 NRL season|NRL Minor Premiers]] – [[Melbourne Storm]]
2016 – Runner-up [[2016 NRL Grand Final|NRL Grand Final]] – [[Melbourne Storm]] ==Coaching career== On 11 June 2021, Green was appointed as the head coach for the inaugural Newcastle Knights [[NRL Women's Premiership|NRLW]] team, one of the 3 new clubs competing in [[2021 NRL Women's season|2021 NRLW Premiership]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news/2021/06/11/a-new-dawn-knights-to-enter-nrlw-in-2021/|title=A New Dawn: Knights to enter NRLW in 2021|date=11 June 2021|website=Newcastle Knights}} Ultimately, Green never had the opportunity to coach the Knights' NRLW team, instead accepting a role with the Knights' NRL team before the delayed [[2021 NRL Women's season]] commenced.{{cite web |last1=Newton |first1=Alicia |title=How Green's blueprint continues to influence Knights |url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2022/02/17/how-greens-blueprint-continues-to-influence-knights/ |website=NRL.com |publisher=[[National Rugby League]] |access-date=10 June 2022 |date=17 February 2022}} On 3 November 2025, the Cowboys announced that Green joined the coaching staff as an assistant. {{Cite web |date=2025-11-02 |title=Cowboys bolster Football Department for 2026 |url=https://www.cowboys.com.au/news/2025/11/03/cowboys-bolster-football-department-for-2026/ |access-date=2025-11-11 |website=North Queensland Cowboys |language=en}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[https://www.warriors.kiwi/teams/telstra-premiership/warriors/blake-green/ New Zealand Warriors profile] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20080327173251/http://www.sharks.com.au/index.php?id=749 Cronulla Sharks profile] *[http://nrl.com/Players/tabid/10345/default.aspx?playerid=309 NRL profile]{{dead link|date=November 2016|bot=InternetArchiveBot|fix-attempted=yes }} *[http://www.news.com.au/heraldsun/story/0,21985,25901152-5016541,00.html Herald Sun | Breaking News from Melbourne and Victoria | Herald Sun] {{Wigan Warriors -
